coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from coix seed for the determination of furazolidone and temperature
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shen
Wang, Zixin
Pang, Yating
Jing, Zerong
Li, Zheng
Peng, Fangfang
Zhao, Yiyan
Guo, Yuyu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, blue emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated by using the hydrothermal strategy from coix seed for the first time. We found that the prepared CDs possessed many excellent characteristics including excitation-dependent properties, good solubility and strong photostability. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of CDs were 363 and 435 nm, respectively. Unbelievably, the fluorescence of CDs was selectively and effectively quenched with the addition of furazolidone (Fu). The quenching mechanisms might be assumed to the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this principle, a novel fluorescence probe was developed for the determination of Fu. At the same time, the proposed probe showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Fu with a wide linear range from 0.5 to 100 μM, and the corresponding detection limit was 0.096 μM. Moreover, the CDs also could be applied for the sensing of temperature. The practical application of the CDs for Fu detection in real samples was also confirmed with the satisfactory recoveries changing from 96.6% to 108.5%, which provided huge possibility in the field of medical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterials
Carbon dots
Furazolidone
Fluorescence detection

Fluorescence quantum yield of CDs measurement
Determination of Fu in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257), the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2104 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2020L0499) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of (A) dosage of coix seed (changing from 0.01 to 0.20 g), (B) reaction temperature (changing from 160 to 200 °C) and (C) reaction time (changing from 1 to 6 h).
(A) TEM image of CDs. (B) The size distribution histogram of CDs.
(A) XPS spectra of CDs; (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s and (D) O 1 s of CDs; (E) FT-IR spectra of CDs.

(A) The UV–Vis (black line), optimal fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (green line) spectra of CDs (inset: photograph of CDs under daylight lamp and UV lamp (365 nm)). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength (varying from 345 to 375 nm).
(A) The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and CDs + Fu mixture; (B) The linear relationship between F0/F value and the concentrations of Fu (5–30 μM).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fu (black line), the fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (green line) spectra of CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs under different concentrations of Fu (changing from 0 to 30.0 μM); (C) Suppressed efficiency of observed (black line) and corrected (red line) fluorescence intensity of CDs with the addition of Fu.
The effect of storage time (A), UV irradiation time (B) and concentration of NaCl on the FL intensity of CDs.
(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs solutions with various Fu concentrations; (B) The linear relationship between ln(F0/F) value and the concentrations of Fu (0.5–100 μM).

The relative fluorescence intensities (F0/F) of CDs solution with Fu, different ions (A) and different small molecules (B); Interference experiments of CDs in the presence of different ions (C) and different small molecules (D).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under different temperature (from 15 to 80 °C); (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and temperature (15–60 °C); (C) The cycles of CDs for the sensing of temperature between 15 °C and 80 °C; (D) The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs at 15 and 80 °C.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and the detection of furazolidone.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of Fu on the of CDs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for Fu determination.
Sensing of Fu in bovine serum samples.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots from coix seed for the determination of furazolidone and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue emissive carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by using a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs were used to selectively detect Fu and with a linear range of 0.5–100 μM on the basis of the static quenching and inner filter effect. Moreover, the CDs also could be applied for the sensing of temperature.

In this work, blue emission fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were fabricated by using the hydrothermal strategy from coix seed for the first time. We found that the prepared CDs possessed many excellent characteristics including excitation-dependent properties, good solubility and strong photostability. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of CDs were 363 and 435 nm, respectively. Unbelievably, the fluorescence of CDs was selectively and effectively quenched with the addition of furazolidone (Fu). The quenching mechanisms might be assumed to the static quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). Based on this principle, a novel fluorescence probe was developed for the determination of Fu. At the same time, the proposed probe showed excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Fu with a wide linear range from 0.5 to 100 μM, and the corresponding detection limit was 0.096 μM. Moreover, the CDs also could be applied for the sensing of temperature. The practical application of the CDs for Fu detection in real samples was also confirmed with the satisfactory recoveries changing from 96.6% to 108.5%, which provided huge possibility in the field of medical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Furazolidone (Fu, Fig. S1), N-(5-nitro-2-furfurylidene)-3-amino-2-oxazolidone, is the most representative nitrofuran antibiotic [1,2]. It has been widely applied for the treatment of gastrointestinal infections and other bacterial livestock diseases [3,4]. For instance, Fu is mainly used to treat various intestinal infections, bacillary dysentery, diarrhea, enteritis, typhoid fever, cholera, infectious rhinitis, blackhead, trichomoniasis, and leukocytosis [5–8]. It can also be combined with antacids and other drugs to treat gastritis caused by Helicobacter pylori. Although Fu has advantages of the above characteristics, excessive use of Fu will cause some side reactions, such as liver and kidney damage, allergic reactions [9,10]. Then, it is very important to accurately detect the concentration of Fu. Up to now, high-performance liquid chromatography [11,12], liquid chromatograph mass spectrometer (LC-MS) [13–15], liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [16,17], spectrophotometry [18,19], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) [20], electrochemical methods [21–24] and fluorescence methods [25] have been successfully developed to analyze Fu. Among them, HPLC and GC–MS have advantages of high sensitivity and low detection limits, they possess shortcoming of the costly instruments, trained operators and time-consuming [26,27]. Electrochemical methods demands cumbersome electrochemical probes and its selectivity is relatively poor. Compared with these traditional analysis technologies, fluorescence methods have attracted tremendous interest due to their inherent advantages of simplicity, high selectivity and sensitivity.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted extensive interest because of their optical electrochemical, biological and physical properties, including strong fluorescence, good biocompatibility, large Stokes shifts, low toxicity, high photostability, high salt resistance, good catalytic activity and pH sensitivity [28–32]. Until now, various types of carbon dots have been successfully established by using hydrothermal methods, such as N,S co-doped carbon dots [33], N-doped carbon quantum dots [34], Cu-doped carbon dots [35], P, N-doped carbon quantum dot [36], MnO2@carbon dots [37], amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots [38] and so on. At the same time, these CDs have been successfully applied for the determination of different substances, such as Hg2+ [39], Fe3+ [40], Cr6+ [41], Cd2+ [42], Cu2+ [43], TNT [44], dopamine [45], hemoglobin [46], cysteine [47], ellagic acid [48] and so on. Fascinatingly, due to its low price and easy availability, many natural biomass as carbon sources have been successfully applied for the synthesis of CDs including soymilk, orange juice, egg, watermelon peel, lemon juice, garlic, cocoon silk and cabbage [49–52]. Coix seed is an edible cereal and is the earliest food mankind has been consuming. Coix seed is rich in fats and carbohydrates and hence is a good source of naturally occurring carbon and nitrogen which is abundant in nature. As far as we know, there are rarely literature reports on the use of coix seed-carbon dots to detect Fu and temperature.

In this paper, for the first time, we described a novel carbon dots (CDs) from coix seed by using hydrothermal methods. Significantly, the fluorescence of the prepared-CDs could be selectively quenched by Fu. Based on this phenomenon, a sensing system for efficient Fu determination was firstly established. Furthermore, the quenching mechanisms were systematically investigated through related analytical characterization. The schematic illustration of the CDs synthesis and the detection of Fu were demonstrated in Scheme 1 .

All reagents were AR grade and obtained without further purification. The coix seed was obtained from supermarket and dried under 50 °C for 14 h, then dried coix seed was crushed into powder through grinder. KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaHSO3, glutamic acid (Gla), glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), histidine (His), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose (Glu) and furazolidone (Fu) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water (18.25 M·cm−1) was purified using a Millipore Milli-Q and employed to prepare different solutions.

The fluorescence characterizations of CDs were collected on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo Japan). Meanwhile, the Ex/Em slits were set as 10.0/10.0 nm and the scanning speed was 1200 nm·min−1. Fluorescence lifetime data were obtained with an FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steady-state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The Shimadzu 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was applied to record the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) data were acquired on a FTIR-8400S (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan). The FEI Tecnai F30 (United States) was employed to analyze transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with the voltage of 200 kV. The ESCALAB 250XI (United States, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was used to take out the characterizations of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The pH value of all solutions was measured through FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China).

For the first time, a novel carbon dots was established by using the facile hydrothermal methods. Briefly, the crushed coix seed (0.05 g) were dispersed in 30 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 5 min, then, the turbid liquid was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave. After incubating under 180 °C for 3 h, the hydrothermal kettle was cooled to room temperature, and the yellow solutions were collected. Finally, the above yellow solutions were filtered and purified to remove larger particles at room temperature for 24 h, respectively. The CDs solutions were stored under 4 °C for further use.

First of all, different Fu concentrations were added into 2.0 mL of PBS (pH = 6.5, 100.0 μg/mL CDs) mixture through a micro pipette of (0–20 μL), respectively. After reacting at room temperature for 60 s, the fluorescence spectra and corresponding intensity of the mixture were obtained at the emission wavelength of 435 nm (the Ex/Em slits were set at 10.0/10.0 nm, respectively).

Furthermore, to investigate the fluorescence selectivity of the prepared-CDs towards Fu, some different possible interferes were added into CDs solution and the corresponding FL intensities were acquired under same sensing conditions, respectively. The specific analysis procedures were established as follows: different possible interferes (10.0 μL, 0.01 M) were separately added into 2.0 mL of PBS (pH = 6.5, 100.0 μg/mL CDs) mixture, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, HSO3 -, Gla, Gly, Ala, Tyr, Pro, His, Ser, Thr, GSH, BSA and Glu, the above mixture were thoroughly incubated at room temperature for 60 s, respectively. Finally, the Fu solutions (10.0 μL, 0.01 M) were dropped into the above solutions containing different possible interferences for the interference experiment, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fu in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The feasibility of the CDs for Fu detection in bovine serum samples was investigated. The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Afterwards, different added concentrations of Fu (10, 30 and 50 μM) were dropped into the bovine serum samples, and the fluorescence intensity and recovery tests were acquired from follow-up experiments. The recoveries were calculated according to formula (1):
(1)
R e c o v e r y =
Measured
concentrations
of
Fu
Added
concentrations
of
Fu

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of CDs measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum yield of CDs was calculated using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard reference [29]. The QY of CDs was determined by the following formula
(2)
φ x
=
φ s
×
A s
A x
×
I x
I s
×
η
x
2
η
s
2
Where I, η, φ and A stood for the integral area of fluorescence intensity, refractive index of the solvent, quantum yield and absorbance, respectively. The × and s stood for object and reference, respectively. The QY of as-prepared CDs was 17.43%.

We first developed a novel carbon dots (CDs) with coix seed as the carbon source and ultra-pure water as the solvent through a facile hydrothermal method (Scheme 1). The effect of preparation conditions on the FL intensity of the CDs was investigated including dosage of coix seed, reaction temperature and reaction time. The final evaluation index was acquired through FL intensity at the emission wavelength of 435 nm. Firstly, we studied the influence of dosage of coix seed on the FL intensity of CDs (changing from 0.01 to 0.20 g). As can be seen in Fig. 1 A, with increasing the dosage of coix seed from 0.01 to 0.05 g, the FL intensity of CDs increased significantly. However, the FL intensity rapidly decreased accompanying the dosage was greater than 0.05 g, which was mainly assumed to the aggregation of CDs under high concentrations. The effect of dilution times on the FL intensity of CDs (0.2 g) was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. S2, as the dilution times increased, the fluorescence intensity of CDs significantly enhanced. Thus, we confirmed that the increase of carbon source concentration leaded to the aggregation of carbon dots. This phenomenon was corresponded with fluorescent photos (inset Fig. 1A). So we selected 0.05 g as the optimal dosage of coix seed for further experiments. Moreover, the effect of reaction temperature on the FL intensity was also investigated changing from 160 to 200 °C (Fig. 1B). When the reaction temperature increased from 160 to 180 °C, the FL intensity increased slowly. After continuing to increase the temperature, the FL intensity was gradually decreasing. Then we chose 180 °C as the optimal reaction temperature for further experiments. Finally, we studied the reaction time towards the influence of FL intensity varying from 1 to 6 h (Fig. 1C). The FL intensity of CDs increased with increasing reaction time from 1 to 3 h. When the reaction time was larger than 3 h, the FL intensity decreased slowly. Then, 3 h was selected the optimal reaction time.

The fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, TEM, FT-IR and XPS were systematically applied for the study of the optical properties, structure, element composition and element state of CDs, respectively.

First of all, the morphology characteristics and size distribution of the as-prepared CDs were taken out through TEM and the images were suggested in Fig. 2 . Obviously, the CDs were well dispersed and there were no particle aggregation in aqueous solution (Fig. 2A). Fig. 2B demonstrated that the size distribution of CDs was relatively small and mainly distributed in the range of 2.1–2.7 nm. According to the statistical results, the average diameter of CDs was 2.4 ± 0.2 nm.
Hereafter, XPS and FT-IR were continuously employed to analyze the elemental composition, elemental state and surface functional groups of the as-prepared CDs.

As we can see in Fig. 3 A, the full spectrum of XPS suggested that three main peaks were found at 284.3, 400.2 and 529.8 eV, which were corresponded with C1s, N1s and O1s of the CDs, respectively. The spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3B) was separated into three peaks, which were located at 284.46 eV (CC), 285.83 eV (CN) and 287.57 eV (OCO). The spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3C) was divided into two peaks at 399.42 eV (CNC) and 399.80 eV (NH) [53]. The spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3D) was separated into three peaks, which were distributed in 531.28 eV (CO), 532.23 eV (OH) and 532.96 eV (C-OH/C-O-C) [54]. As we can see from the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 3E), the transmission peak around 3367 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of OH and NH. The transmission peaks around 2933, 1670, 1532 and 1042 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of CH2/CH3, CO, CC and COC. The bending vibration absorption peaks of NH/COO and CO/CN at 1400 cm−1 and 1192 cm−1 was observed in CDs [55], respectively. Based on the analysis results of XPS and FT-IR, many oxygen-containing active functional groups could be significantly observed on the surface of the CDs, which was the main reason of excellent stability and water solubility.

Finally, the optical performances of the CDs were also investigated through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively. As we can see in Fig. 4 A, there was an obvious absorption peak at 350 nm. The maximum of emission wavelength of CDs (435 nm) was obtained at the maximum excitation wavelength of 363 nm, and the CDs solution suggested pale-yellow and bright blue under daylight lamp and UV lamp (365 nm), respectively (inset Fig. 4A). At the same time, the effect of the fluorescence excitation wavelengths on the fluorescence emission wavelengths was also discussed. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the FL intensities and the position of peaks constantly varied with increasing the fluorescence excitation wavelength from 345 to 375 nm, illustrating that the prepared-CDs possessed excitation-dependent properties. The above analysis results suggested that the prepared-CDs were successfully developed through this facile and rapid strategy (Scheme 1).

As far as we know, according to the interaction between fluorescent agent and quencher, the static quenching and dynamic quenching were usually employed for the study of the fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetimes were applied to discuss the quenching mechanism between CDs and CDs + Fu mixture. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, the lifetimes of CDs and CDs + Fu mixture were 4.94 and 4.89 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime difference did not exceed 0.1 ns without and with Fu, which proved that the quenching mechanism might be attributed to the static quenching instead of dynamic quenching. To further verify the static quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation (3) [56] was used to discuss our conjecture.
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
Where, F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs + Fu mixture, respectively. K SV refers to the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] represents to the amounts of Fu, and τ0 represents the lifetime of the prepared-CDs (4.94 ns). As illustrated in Fig. 5B, 2.2 × 104 L·mol−1 and 4.46 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1 were the corresponding calculation results of K SV and K q, respectively. The K q was significantly greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1), which proved that the quenching mechanism should be the static quenching [26,57].

To further investigate the possible mechanism, the relationship between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fu and the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs was first studied. As we can see in Fig. 6 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fu obviously overlapped the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, which was evidence of the inner filter effect (IFE) [58]. Furthermore, the Parker equation (4) was applied for the study the above results.
(4)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
Where, Fobsd refers to the recorded fluorescence intensity, Fcor represents to the corrected fluorescence intensity when the IFE is excluded. Aex and Aem are the absorbance at the optimal excitation and emission wavelength. s is the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.1 cm). g and d are the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette and the width of the cuvette (g = 0.4 cm, d = 1.0 cm), respectively. The value of Aex and Aem were read from Fig. 6B, and the Fcor/Fobsd, Ecor and Eobsd were calculated through the Parker equation (4) and displayed in Table 1 and Fig. 6C. The value of Fcor/Fobsd and the suppressed efficiency of observed (Eobsd) gradually increased, when the Fu concentrations increased from 0 to 30 μM. Meanwhile, the value of Eobsd was greater than the Ecor, which confirmed that the mechanism was ascribed to the IFE [59–62].

In order to explore more application areas, we first investigated the stability of CDs under different conditions, such as storage time, UV irradiation time and the concentrations of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 7 A, after the storage time of 16 d, no significant weakening about FL intensity was observed. As we can see from Fig. 7B, the FL intensity of CDs remained stable after the UV irradiation time of 10 min. Moreover, the FL intensity of CDs was stable when the NaCl concentration reached 0.5 M (Fig. 7C). Thence, the CDs possessed excellent stability.

In order to achieve the optimal detection effect of CDs for Fu, pH value and incubation time were discussed. The influence of the pH value on the FL intensity of CDs for Fu was first discussed varying from 6.0 to 8.0. As shown in Fig. S3, with increasing the pH value from 6.0 to 6.5, the F0-F value of CDs + Fu system gradually increased. When the pH value was greater than 6.5, the F0-F value decreased. Furthermore, we studied the effect of reaction time for the FL intensity change of the detection system. As can be seen in Fig. S4, the F0-F value reached the maximum after the reaction time of 60 s. Thus, we selected 6.5 and 60 s as the optimal pH value and reaction time.

Herein, to investigate the selectivity of the developed probe towards Fu, the effects of various potentially interfering substances, including different ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, SO4 2-, SO3 2- and HSO3 -) and different small molecules (Gla, Gly, Ala, Tyr, Pro, His, Ser, Thr, GSH, BSA and Glu) on the FL intensity of the CDs probe were studied. As seen in Fig. 9 A and B, only Fu generated an obviously effect on the FL of CDs, while the other species had negligible influence on the probes. Furthermore, fluorescence quenching still remained stable in the coexistence of Fu and these interfering species (Fig. 9C and D), indicating the excellent anti-interference of this proposed sensor for Fu determination. The above experiment results suggested that the CDs could be used to distinguish Fu from other substances and possessed high selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fu in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the proposed probe was demonstrated through the detection of Fu in bovine serum samples. Before the experiments, there was no Fu determined in the bovine serum samples. Afterwards, spiked samples were obtained through addition the Fu into real samples. As shown in Table 3 , it was clear that the measured values were very close to the spiked values of Fu, and the recoveries of Fu changed from 96.6% to 108.5%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were below. The results suggested that the proposed probe possessed a bright future in analysis fields.

The possibility of the prepared-CDs for the detection of temperature was investigated. We studied the effect of different temperature on the FL intensity of CDs varying from 15 to 80 °C. As can be seen in Fig. 10 A, the FL intensity of CDs decreased gradually with increasing temperature from 15 to 80 °C, which was the reason of the increase of molecular collision frequency and non-radiative transition rate, while the radiation transition rate at high temperature remained unchanged. Then it reduced emission intensity of excited state [63,64]. Obviously, a linear curve was obtained between the FL intensity and the temperature varying from 15 to 60 °C (Fig. 10B). The corresponding linear fitting equation was F = −11.29[Tem] + 1745.03 (R2 = 0.9952). Unexpectedly, the FL intensity of CDs could be recovered at low temperature (Fig. 10C). As displayed in Fig. 10D, the lifetimes of CDs at 15 and 80 °C were 4.69 and 4.46 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime difference did not exceed 1.0 ns at 15 and 80 °C, which proved that the quenching mechanism might be attributed to the static quenching. This proposed sensor could be duplicated for five uninterrupted cycles and the FL intensity only changed slightly.

In summary, a green and blue emissive carbon dots was proposed through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method without addition of any additives. The CDs possessed excellent stability after long time storage and UV irradiation and high NaCl concentrations. Notably, the FL of CDs could be quenched by Fu. The static quenching and inner filter effect were proved to the quenching mechanism. Based on this phenomenon, a detection platform for Fu was established and a linear curve was taken out in the range of 0.5–100 µM. The lower detection limit could reach 0.096 µM. In addition, the synthesized-CDs could also be used to detect temperature. Meanwhile, according to the satisfactory recoveries, this CDs possessed great prospects in practical applications.

Shen Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Zixin Wang: Investigation. Yating Pang: Investigation. Zerong Jing: Investigation. Zheng Li: Software. Fangfang Peng: Software. Yiyan Zhao: Data curation. Yuyu Guo: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257), the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2104) and the Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2020L0499) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119969.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Martindale, The Complete Drug Reference, thirty fourth ed., Pharmaceutical Press, 2005, pp. 605–609.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Greenwood, Antimicrobial Chemotherapy, 13th ed., Oxford, 1995, pp. 128-130.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of furazolidone in human biological fluids by high performance liquid chromatography

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemistry of the interaction of furazolidone and bovine serum albumin

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective and sensitive furazolidone biosensor based on DNA-modified TiO2-reduced graphene oxide

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Forensic issues in the analysis of trace nitrofuran veterinary residues in food of animal origin

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of furazolidone in porcine tissue using thermospray liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and a study of the pharmacokinetics and stability of its residues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. JohnáBlanchflower, D. GlennáKennedy, Determination of furazolidone in porcine tissue using thermospray liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry and a study of the pharmacokinetics and stability of its residues, Analyst 120 (1995) 2347-2351.
